meta.title: Hong Kong Civil Law Relationship Graph
notes: Primary Hong Kong Civil Law judgments were fetched from HKLII's public API.
Supplemental textbook passages were used to improve topic coverage and backfill missing authorities.
NotebookLM is optional in this build; uncovered topics are surfaced in monitor_report.json for later enrichment.
Curated lineage paths add Hong Kong and UK authority sequences with FLLW, APPD, DIST, and CODI edges.

members.label: Investors Compensation Scheme Ltd v West Bromwich Building Society
members.note: Originating UK authority for Lord Hoffmann's contextual principles.

members.label: Sinoearn International Ltd v Hyundai-CCECC Joint Venture
members.note: Hong Kong CFA endorsement of the contextual approach.

members.label: Rainy Sky SA v Kookmin Bank
members.note: Commercial common-sense peak in the UK line.

members.label: Fully Profit (Asia) Ltd v Secretary for Justice
members.note: Hong Kong treatment following Rainy Sky.

members.label: Arnold v Britton
members.note: Textualist correction emphasizing contractual language.

members.label: Sany Heavy Industry (HK) Ltd v Sany-Kenton (HK) Ltd
members.note: Recent Hong Kong application of the textualist correction.

members.label: Wood v Capita
members.note: Modern unitary synthesis balancing text and context.

members.label: Stilk v Myrick
members.note: Traditional existing-duty rule.

members.label: Williams v Roffey Bros & Nicholls
members.note: UK practical-benefit exception to the traditional rule.

members.label: UBC (Construction) Ltd v Sung Foo Kee Ltd
members.note: Hong Kong adoption of Williams v Roffey.

members.label: Re Selectmove
members.note: Refused to extend practical benefit to part-payment of debt.

members.label: Chong Cheng Lin Courtney v Cathay Pacific Airways Ltd
members.note: Hong Kong follow-on treatment of the Selectmove limitation.

members.label: MWB Business Exchange Centres Ltd v Rock Advertising Ltd
members.note: Illustrates continuing caution in this area.

members.label: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v New Garage & Motor Co Ltd
members.note: Classic genuine pre-estimate versus deterrent framing.

members.label: Cavendish Square Holding BV v El Makdessi
members.note: Modern legitimate-interest and proportionality test.

members.label: Law Ting Pong Secondary School v Chen Wai-mick
members.note: Hong Kong adoption of the Makdessi test.

members.label: China Great Wall AMC (International) Holdings Co Ltd v Royal-Investment Group Corp
members.note: Hong Kong application refining the Law Ting Pong line.

members.label: Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge (No 2)
members.note: Set the modern bank-procedure approach to constructive notice.

members.label: Li Sau Keung v Bank of China (Hong Kong) Ltd
members.note: Hong Kong follow-on application.

members.label: Unconscionable Contracts Ordinance (Cap. 458)
members.note: Statutory foundation of the Hong Kong doctrine.

members.label: Shum Kong v Chan Man-fai
members.note: Seminal Hong Kong interpretation of the Ordinance.

members.label: Hongkong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd
members.note: Foundational innominate-term authority with Hong Kong facts.

members.label: Billion Star Development Ltd v Wong Tak-fai
members.note: Modern Hong Kong application on termination rights.

members.label: Donoghue v Stevenson
members.note: House of Lords — neighbour principle; duty of care owed to those who ought reasonably to be in contemplation as affected by acts or omissions.

members.label: Anns v Merton London Borough Council
members.note: Two-stage test: sufficient proximity? Any policy reasons to limit duty? Enabled significant expansion of duty.

members.label: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman
members.note: House of Lords — three-stage test: (1) foreseeable harm, (2) proximity, (3) fair just and reasonable. Replaced Anns incrementalism.

members.label: Luen Hing Fat Cooked Food Stall v Yau Tsim Mong District Board
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal applied Caparo three-stage test as the applicable framework.

lineages.title: Judicial Review: From Wednesbury to Proportionality
topic_labels: Basic Law and Bill of Rights

members.label: Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd v Wednesbury Corporation
members.note: Court of Appeal — unreasonableness: decision so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it.

members.label: Council of Civil Service Unions v Minister for Civil Service
members.note: House of Lords — three grounds of review: illegality, irrationality, procedural impropriety.

members.label: Hysan Development Co Ltd v Town Planning Board
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal — proportionality test applies for BOR/Basic Law rights; Wednesbury applies for non-rights matters.

members.label: Leung Kwok Hung v HKSAR
members.note: Court of Final Appeal applied proportionality to freedom of assembly restrictions under Basic Law.

lineages.title: Unfair Prejudice and Minority Shareholder Protection

members.label: O'Neill v Phillips
members.note: House of Lords — unfair prejudice requires conduct unfair in a commercial sense; legitimate expectations from informal understanding enforceable.

members.label: Re Kong Thai Sawmill (Miri) Sdn Bhd
members.note: Hong Kong Privy Council — just and equitable winding up; loss of mutual confidence in small quasi-partnership company.

members.label: Re Kam Kwan Lai
members.note: Hong Kong Court of First Instance — applied Companies Ordinance s.724 unfair prejudice; buyout remedy ordered.

members.label: Re Chime Communications Ltd
members.note: Court of Appeal — scope of unfair prejudice petition under Cap.622; management exclusion and valuation principles.

lineages.title: Ancillary Relief: From Discretion to Equality

members.label: White v White
members.note: House of Lords — yardstick of equality as check on discretion; no bias against non-money earner; outcome should not discriminate.

members.label: Miller v Miller; McFarlane v McFarlane
members.note: House of Lords — sharing principle, compensation principle, and needs; sharing based on equal partnership of marriage.

members.label: LKW v DD
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Final Appeal — adopted White/Miller framework; equal sharing as starting point; departure requires good reason.

members.label: SPH v SA
members.note: Hong Kong Court of Appeal applied LKW v DD — factors justifying departure from equal sharing in long and short marriages.

lineage_codes.FLLW: Followed or adopted in Hong Kong

meta.viewer_intro_public: A Hong Kong Civil Law hierarchy and authority network built from HKLII judgments plus local textbook passages. Start at the doctrinal modules, then drill into topics, cases, and statutes.

principles.statement_en: The Eshelby rule, derived from English law, addresses the amendment of pleadings to add claims for sums that became due after the writ was issued, where the original claim was for an instalment due before the writ.

principles.label_en: Hong Kong approach to amendments curing defects
principles.statement_en: Hong Kong law, following Wing Siu, does not permit an amendment to cure a defective claim by adding a necessary factual averment that could not have been pleaded as at the date of the writ.

The court's summary power to strike out a pleading should only be exercised in plain and obvious cases; where the legal viability of a cause of action is sensitive to the facts, a strike-out order is inappropriate.

principles.label_en: Overruling of Penrose principle on service knowledge
principles.statement_en: The principle established in Penrose Industries Ltd v Tam Yan Lung—that a defendant who acquires actual knowledge of proceedings cannot challenge the regularity or effectiveness of service—has been overruled by the Court of Appeal.

For service to be valid under Order 10, rule 1(2) of the Rules of the High Court, the plaintiff must demonstrate that the defendant was residing at the address at the time of service, and mere temporary absence does not invalidate service if the defendant ordinarily resides there.

principles.label_en: Distinction between service in Hong Kong and outside jurisdiction
principles.statement_en: The requirements for substituted service differ depending on whether the defendant is believed to be residing in Hong Kong or outside the jurisdiction; prior unsuccessful attempts at service under Order 11, rule 5A are necessary only when the defendant is outside Hong Kong.

principles.statement_en: For service of a writ under Order 10 to be valid, the defendant must be within the jurisdiction at the time the writ is served, not merely at the time it is placed in a letterbox.

principles.label_en: Proof of subsequent knowledge and presence
principles.statement_en: A plaintiff may prove valid service by showing that the defendant subsequently came within the jurisdiction and gained knowledge of the writ, thereby curing an initial irregularity.

principles.label_en: Invalidity of service without required affidavit
principles.statement_en: Service of a writ is invalid if the plaintiff fails to file the affidavit required by Order 10 rule 1(3)(b)(i) when the writ could not have come to the defendant's knowledge within seven days.
A defendant's actual knowledge of proceedings alone does not validate service that was irregular because the defendant was outside the jurisdiction at the time of service.

principles.label_en: Consideration of 'unless the contrary is shown'
principles.statement_en: When assessing service validity, the court must consider the phrase 'unless the contrary is shown' in Order 10 rule 1(3), which relates to the date of service, not the defendant's location.

principles.statement_en: The court will not grant leave to serve proceedings out of jurisdiction unless the plaintiff satisfies it that Hong Kong is clearly and distinctly the more appropriate forum than the alternative, being the forum with the most real and substantial connection to the action.

principles.label_en: Good arguable case requirement for service out
principles.statement_en: A plaintiff seeking leave to serve proceedings out of jurisdiction must demonstrate a good arguable case on the merits of its claim.
A foreign judgment on the merits can found a defence of res judicata in Hong Kong proceedings, precluding re-litigation of the same issues.
Applying to strike out proceedings for lack of jurisdiction does not, in itself, constitute a submission to the jurisdiction of the court.

Instituting proceedings in Hong Kong on matters that have already been litigated and decided against the plaintiff in a foreign court can constitute an abuse of process and cause hardship to the defendants.

principles.label_en: Submission to jurisdiction dispenses with service for contempt
principles.statement_en: When a party has submitted to the jurisdiction of the court in the main action, personal service of subsequent contempt proceedings arising from that action is not required, even if the party is outside the jurisdiction.

principles.label_en: Contempt as incidental to court's jurisdiction
principles.statement_en: Once a court has established jurisdiction over a person in a claim, that jurisdiction extends to matters incidental to the claim, including enforcement proceedings such as contempt of court.

principles.label_en: Discretion to dispense with service in contempt
principles.statement_en: The court has a discretion to dispense with the requirement to serve an originating summons for contempt proceedings.
Contempt proceedings should only be invoked as a measure of last resort.
An order cannot be enforced by committal for contempt unless its terms are expressed with sufficient clarity.

principles.statement_en: For an action to be registrable as a pending land action (lis pendens), it must claim a proprietary right in the land itself, not merely seek to restrain the owner from disposing of the property.

principles.label_en: Lien over shares vs. proprietary interest in land
principles.statement_en: A claim to a lien over the shares of a company does not constitute a claim to a proprietary interest in the company's land.

principles.label_en: Injunction does not create proprietary interest
principles.statement_en: An injunction sought to enforce a lien (which is not itself a proprietary interest in land) does not transform that lien into a proprietary interest in land for the purposes of constituting a lis pendens.

principles.label_en: Good reason for writ renewal - innocent mistake
principles.statement_en: A failure to properly serve a writ within the required time period, if it results from an innocent mistake, can constitute a 'good reason' justifying the court's discretion to renew the writ's validity.
Neglect by a solicitor or their managing clerk, leading to a failure to serve a writ within time, can be a valid basis for a court to exercise its discretion to extend the writ's validity.

principles.label_en: Mistaken service after expiry as good reason
principles.statement_en: A mistaken belief that a writ has been properly served, when in fact its validity had expired, can constitute a 'good reason' for renewal.
The principle that an innocent mistake constitutes a good reason for renewing a writ applies even where the relevant limitation period for the action has expired.

principles.label_en: Proper service on company registered address
principles.statement_en: Serving a writ on a limited company's registered office is a normal and acceptable form of service, and is not improper even if the company's solicitors have requested future correspondence be sent to them, unless those solicitors specifically state they have authority to accept service.

principles.statement_en: When determining whether a good reason has been shown for failing to serve a writ within the required time, it is relevant to consider if the non-service resulted from the plaintiff's deliberate choice. If it was a choice, there must be a good reason justifying that deliberate non-compliance with the time limit.

On an application to set aside an ex parte extension of a writ's validity, the court may consider not only the evidence presented at the time of the extension application but also evidence that subsequently comes to light.

It is an error of law to use evidence of events occurring after the date of an application to extend a writ's validity to reassess and nullify a 'good reason' that was prima facie established at the time of the application. This misapplies the principle allowing consideration of subsequent evidence.

references.snippet: IN THE HIGH COURT OF THE HONG KONG SPECIAL ADMINISTRATIVE REGION COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE Topics: Writs and Originating Summonses.

principles.label_en: Valid service of writ requires presence in jurisdiction
principles.statement_en: For service of a writ to be valid under Hong Kong rules, the defendant must be physically within the jurisdiction at the time of service. Service attempted when the defendant is outside the jurisdiction is invalid.

principles.label_en: Forum non conveniens first hurdle - natural forum
principles.statement_en: In a forum non conveniens application, the defendant must first establish that there is another available forum which is clearly more appropriate for the trial. If this first hurdle is not overcome, the court need not consider the comparative appropriateness of alternative forums.

principles.label_en: Joinder of claims for interests of justice
principles.statement_en: Where claims against multiple defendants are factually and legally inseparable, with the liability of some defendants dependent on the establishment of claims against a primary defendant, it is in the interests of justice for all claims to be determined together in a single proceeding.

When a witness is imprisoned overseas and may be extradited, video conferencing facilities may be the only practical method to secure their live testimony for trial, regardless of where the trial is held.
